# Preface {.unnumbered}

::::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
:::: {.hero-band}
::: {.hero-lead}
Social science now has to be reproducible, and reproducibility
runs at the shell. This is the terminal book for economists and
for finance and policy researchers: the fluency to drive your own
analysis from the command line, and to stay in charge of it in an
age when an AI will happily type the commands for you.
:::

[Start at Chapter 1](chapters/01-why-terminal.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}
[How to read a command](chapters/04-shell-literacy.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
[Command cheat sheet](cheatsheet.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
::::
:::::

Empirical social science has spent the past decade learning that
a finding is only as trustworthy as its rebuild, and being able
to produce that rebuild on demand is now an ordinary expectation
rather than an exceptional one. The companion volume, *Git and
GitHub for Social Science Researchers*, makes the case for why,
and this one takes that as settled. Its subject is
the layer just beneath: the shell is where a versioned project
stops being a description of what you did and becomes something a
stranger can actually rerun. Chapter 1 draws that line in full;
the Preface only marks where the two books meet, so that
everything here can point back to reproducibility rather than
re-argue it.

::::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
:::: {.grid .feature-grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4 .feature-card}
#### One real project, run for real

A single asset-pricing study threads through every chapter. Its
data rebuild from a seeded script, its headline result reproduces
under a locked environment, and every command block shows the
output the command actually printed.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4 .feature-card}
#### From first prompt to remote server

Readers range from an undergraduate who has never opened a
terminal to a PhD student moving a job onto a cluster, and
include the practicing researcher or analyst who was never
formally taught the shell. No computer-science background is
assumed. The default machine is a Mac; Linux and Windows
(through WSL2) differences are flagged where they bite.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4 .feature-card}
#### Built for the agentic-AI moment

AI coding agents now sit at the prompt and run commands for you.
This book teaches the literacy that lets you direct them well and
catch them when they are wrong.
:::
::::
:::::

## Why this book exists

I wrote this book because I needed it and it did not exist.
Producing empirical research, I had to assemble command-line
fluency from sources written for system administrators and
software engineers, where the examples are servers and the
anxieties are uptime. The hardest part was never a single command.
It was not knowing what I did not yet know: which habits keep an
analysis rebuildable, which commands quietly destroy work, how to
move a job onto a bigger machine without losing the thread. This
book is the map I wanted then, drawn for researchers.

## It looks intimidating. It repays a career.

There is no use pretending otherwise: the first time you face a
blank prompt and a blinking cursor, the terminal is intimidating.
There are no menus to browse, the error messages are terse, and
the beginner's honest worry, that one wrong keystroke will break
something you cannot get back, is not unfounded. This book does
not wave that worry away. It takes the risk seriously and shows
you where the sharp edges are, in Chapter 1 and then at length in
Chapter 7, rather than pretending they are not there.

What makes the discomfort worth pushing through is how durable
the skill is. Graphical apps are redesigned every few years and
programming languages drift in and out of fashion, but the shell
you learn now has been stable for decades and will still be
driving your pipelines long after today's tools are gone. Almost
nothing else in a research toolkit compounds like that. The
afternoon that feels wasted in the first week is repaid quietly
every week after, for the rest of a working life.

## The skill that makes AI worth using

The reason this matters more now than it did a few years ago is
that the terminal has become the place where AI does its work. A
new generation of coding agents, Claude Code, OpenAI's Codex, and
Google's Gemini among them, does not sit in a chat window off to
the side. It sits at your prompt, runs shell commands, edits your
files, and wires steps into your pipeline. Chapter 2 counts these
agents as a genuine fourth way to get unstuck, alongside a
command's own help, its manual, and the web, and they are good at
it.

But an agent is only as useful as the person steering it. It
drafts an invocation; you still have to know whether it is the
right one. It offers a fix; you still have to see what the fix
would touch. The researchers who get the most from these tools
are not the ones who know the least and lean hardest, but the
ones fluent enough to give a precise instruction and read the
result that comes back. Chapter 17 takes this up in full.

## Why learn the shell when the AI can type it

That raises the honest question of the moment: if an agent can
write the commands, why learn to read them? Because you remain
responsible for what runs on your machine and what goes into your
paper, and an agent cannot carry that responsibility for you. It
will propose a command with the same confidence whether the
command is right or quietly wrong, and the shell does exactly
what it is told, including deleting data the agent misjudged as
disposable. You cannot check what you cannot read. Shell literacy
is what lets you accept the good suggestion, refuse the dangerous
one, and tell them apart before you press return, which is the
whole difference between directing a powerful tool and gambling
with it. AI raises the value of this skill; it does not retire
it. Chapters 2 and 17 return to the judgment this takes; the
Preface only plants the flag.

## The companion volume

This book has a companion, *Git and GitHub for Social Science
Researchers*, written together with this one and designed as its
pair. The division of labor is simple. The companion is about
keeping an honest record of a project: version control,
collaboration on GitHub, and the replication package a journal
will accept. This book is about the layer where the project
actually runs: the shell, the data pipeline, the software
environment, and the remote machine. Each book stands alone and
restates what it needs, so you are never sent away mid-chapter.
Read together, in either order, they cover the reproducible
workflow end to end.

Two boundary topics are split deliberately rather than repeated.
Environments: Chapter 13 of this book builds and activates them
at the shell; Chapter 8 of the companion records them in the
repository through lockfiles. AI tools: Chapter 17 of this book
is about directing an agent at the prompt; Chapter 16 of the
companion is about reviewing what the agent did as diffs.

If you want the full argument for reproducibility, the
companion's Chapter 1 makes it, and this book takes it as
settled. If you have never opened a terminal, you are exactly who
this book's first chapters are for; start here, and the companion
will read more easily afterward.

Chapter 1 lays out how to read the book. One convention, though,
belongs up front, because it shapes every page that follows.

## About the captured output

Every command block in this book that shows a prompt and its
output is a real capture from an actual terminal session, most on
a Linux machine, the macOS and BSD divergences on a Mac, and the
remote examples against a real server; none is typed up from
memory or invented. Where a tool has to be described rather
than run, such as a full-screen program or a Stata or EViews
recipe, the block carries no prompt and is labeled as such, so
you never mistake it for a real capture.

Because the repository is public, values that would identify a
person or a machine are changed before an output is saved: an
account or user name, a home directory, a hostname, and a
network address are each replaced with a bracketed placeholder,
`[account]`, `[hostname]`, `[ip]`, and the like, that your own
machine fills in with the real value. Two other edits are marked
wherever they occur: a very long hash is shortened for
readability (`49b3a1733cf2...`), and a stretch of repetitive or
cache-dependent noise is elided with a bracketed `[...]`. Those
marks aside, every block is exactly what the command printed.
